data.title: Rexford Industrial: Great Opportunity For Long-Term Investors To Buy The Dip
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Dividend Collectuh.

It pays to be a long-term investor — the more time you have in the market, usually the better off you'll be. That's because when stocks take a beating for whatever reason, you can buy the dip and bring down your cost basis. Like the saying goes, “time in the market beats timing the market.”

So, when my holdings offer me a nice opportunity by suffering a nice dip, I get euphoric as I'm able to pick up shares discounted to my average. This is currently happening with Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (NYSE:REXR), a stock I hold in my retirement account.

Mr. Market didn't seem too happy with the company's recent Q3 earnings. In this article, I discuss the company's latest earnings, headwinds, and why REXR may be a compelling buy for long-term investors.

Previous Strong Buy Thesis

I last covered Rexford Industrial this past July, right before the company reported its Q2 earnings. Analysts expected funds from operations, or FFO, of $0.58, in-line with the first quarter. I predicted FFO in a range of $0.59 — $0.61 due to the company's strong leasing activity during the quarter that saw them complete $1.1 billion worth of transactions across 3.2 million square feet.

Their FFO came right in the middle of my prediction at $0.60, beating estimates by $0.02 while revenue of $237.57 million also beat by $1.49 million. Due to the disappointment with their latest earnings, the stock is down over 7% at the time of writing, basically flat from July.

At the same time, the S&P has continued to perform well, up 5.20%.

Not As Bad As It Seems

As previously mentioned, Rexford Industrial reported their Q3 earnings, and the stock was down over $3 a share the day after. Maybe the market didn't like their FFO declining by a penny from the previous quarter?

Despite this, core FFO of $130 million grew double-digits from the previous year. Achieving 13.1% growth during tougher than normal times, especially for REITs due to high interest rates, is actually pretty impressive in my opinion.

But the Southern California market has been facing headwinds all year due to weaker supply and demand. And with REXR's occupancy dropping to 96.9% from 97.3% in the previous quarter, I think this is why their share price is experiencing volatility at the moment.

Year-over-year, this declined from 97.3%. Their largest peer Prologis (PLD) also saw their occupancy dip from the previous year. This stood at 96.2% during their latest earnings, a decline of 1.3% from Q3 '23. So, for comparison purposes, Rexford saw the smaller decline, a testament to the strength of the SoCal market.

However, the latter's property locations in Southern California is something that always seems to worry investors. For me, as a long-term investor, this is a perfect buying opportunity.

The REIT also saw double-digit consolidated portfolio NOI growth of 17.6% to $183.5 million.

Same-property NOI grew a solid 2.6% while cash NOI increased 5.3% but declined from Q3 '23 that saw same-property NOI increase 8.9% and 9.5% on a cash basis. This could also be a contributing factor for the share price decline.

During the quarter, REXR made two acquisitions and one disposition, achieving leasing spreads of 68% on a net effective basis and 49% on a cash-basis. Year-to-date, the REIT has made $1.4 billion in investments, compared to $1.2 billion in 2023.

So, from an operational standpoint, REXR performed well besides the slight drop in occupancy and lower NOI growth. Prologis saw a drop in both metrics as well, with NOI growth on a net effective and cash basis declining from 9.3% and 9.5% to 6.2% and 7.2% respectively. And while headwinds for the sector persist, the market overreacted to what, I thought, was a solid earnings report.

Q3’24

Q3’23

Consolidated Portfolio NOI

$183.5M

$156.1M

Core FFO

$130M

Occupancy

96.9%

97.3%

YTD investments

$1.4B

NOI growth

2.6% / 5.3%

8.9% / 9.5%

Balance Sheet Remains Solid

REXR's investment-grade balance sheet and well-staggered debt maturities positions them well for a lower interest rate environment. Additionally, they had plenty of liquidity available with nearly $1 billion on their revolver and $62 million in cash, more than enough to satisfy their $560 million in debt due next year.

Their leverage was also solid at 4.7x, although this was up from 3.7x the year prior. This is in comparison to 5.1x for peer PLD, who also reported their Q3 earnings the same day as REXR.

Dividend Safety

Through three quarters, Rexford Industrial's FFO saw double-digit growth of 13.07% from $122.2 million to $138.2 million. Using their shares outstanding of 219,133 this gives the REIT a very conservative FFO payout ratio of 66.1%.

Moreover, management raised full-year core FFO guidance by $0.01 on their lower end to $2.32 — $2.34. This represents a solid 6.39% increase from $2.19 in 2023. And despite their adjusted FFO, or AFFO, declining slightly from the previous quarter, their payout ratio was still solid at 84.1%.

Valuation

At a price of $45.48 at the time of writing, this gives REXR a forward P/FFO multiple of 19.51x. This is in comparison to Prologis' 22.7x using their core FFO guidance midpoint of $5.44.

EastGroup Properties' (EGP) P/FFO multiple currently stands at 21.96x using their guidance midpoint of $8.35 while First Industrial's (FR) guidance midpoint of $2.63 gives them a forward multiple of 20.86x.

So, from a valuation standpoint, Rexford Industrial is the cheapest among its peers.

Again, as a long-term investor, I suspect volatility to continue for the near to medium term and for those looking to trade REXR because of their share price decline, I would advise against it. But looking out over the next 2 years, REXR currently offers compelling upside. Plus, you get paid a well-covered dividend while you wait.

Looking out until the end of 2026, Rexford Industrial offers strong upside of 93% if the stock returns to its normal multiple. As you can see above, they currently traded below this (blue line) since early last year.

And while there is no guarantee REXR will return to its historical average, I think it's safe to say they are likely to see upside in the next 12–24 months due to the likelihood of much lower interest rates.

Risks & Bottom Line

As a REIT solely invested in the Southern California infill market, REXR will likely see increased volatility for the near to medium-term due to market softness in the region due to declining supply and demand. But as mentioned previously, it's better to have a long-term outlook and use a buy-and-hold strategy.

If you are a long-term investor, then REXR may present a compelling buying opportunity as the market processes the REIT's latest earnings report. Trading well-below their normal multiple, those with patience may be rewarded handsomely if willing to hold the stock through tough times currently.

Like all great companies, I expect Rexford Industrial to continue seeing strong growth in their earnings while ramping up their investments as interest rates are lowered over the next 12–24 months.

As a result, their earnings and occupancy ratings are also likely to increase, thus positively impacting the share price over time. Despite headwinds, Rexford's fundamentals remain solid, and I continue to rate them a strong buy.

data.title: Stellantis: Free Cash Flow Disaster
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) is grappling with one of its biggest challenges to date—an unprecedented profit warning dated September 30th that paints a bleak picture for the company’s near future. If you’ve been keeping an eye on Stellantis as a potential value investment, I warn you that this isn't just a bump in the road; it’s a massive downgrade that has slashed its adjusted operating income margin for FY24 from over 10% to as low as 5.5%. This is a near 40% downgrade in expected profits​, which came suddenly and aggressively. Such a drastic reduction is unheard of outside of recessionary periods and underscores deep-seated issues. In my view, investors cannot overlook such a sharp margin collapse, as it signals systemic, rather than cyclical, problems in Stellantis’ business operations. For me Stellantis is a clear "Strong Sell", and I wouldn't touch this stock with a 10 foot pole.

Notably, Stellantis shares are now down about 43% for the year, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 21%, and competitor General Motors (GM) is up 27%, respectively.

A Disastrous Profit Warning in 2024 ...

Stellantis' latest profit warning is a stark admission of how badly things have unraveled for the company. Notably, management is slashing its FY24 adjusted operating income margin forecast from "over 10%" to a dismal 5.5%-7%. On that note, I point out that H2 margins are likely barely above break even, after a close to 10% margin in H1. This is no small slip-up. Stellantis has had to ramp up inventory cuts, chopping an additional 200,000 units in the second half of 2024. For a company that prided itself on efficient inventory management, this is an embarrassing setback. The hit to free cash flow is even more shocking—Stellantis is now guiding for a cash outflow between €5 billion and €10 billion​. That’s a jaw-dropping reversal from earlier expectations of a positive cash flow, and it casts serious doubt on the company's ability to maintain its capital allocation plans. Personally, I wouldn't be surprised seeing Stellantis cutting the FY 2025 dividend by >70% vs. FY 2024.

There are a few drivers to this horrible profit warning: Firstly, Stellantis is ramping up efforts to reduce its bloated inventories in the U.S., which has triggered costly incentives and production cuts that have gutted margins. At the same time, North America isn’t the only region where Stellantis is in trouble. While the U.S. has dominated headlines, the situation in Europe is also concerning. Volume declines are hitting hard across the board. Stellantis had been banking on its electrification strategy and new product rollouts to carry it through a challenging market, but these moves now appear grossly insufficient. The company has seen significant shipment downgrades not just in the U.S., but also in Europe and other markets​. Thus, this widespread weakness suggests that Stellantis is struggling with far more than just a North American hiccup.

Stellantis has long "advertised" its ability to manage costs and drive synergies, especially in the wake of the PSA-FCA merger. But given the recent profit warning, investors are - and should be - left wondering whether the company has overpromised and underdelivered on its restructuring plans.

... Coupled With A Bleak Outlook for 2025

With the magnitude of the FY24 warning, the outlook for 2025 is bleak. Even if Stellantis manages to stabilize its North American operations, the company faces a prolonged recovery period. Analysts have already cut their AOI margin estimates for 2025, with a 30% downside risk being floated​ (Source Refinitiv, consensus estimates). Indeed, EBIT estimates for FY 2025 are now at about $12 billion, compared to $23 billion estimated two quarters ago.

Although Stellantis' management was hoping to bounce back quickly after H1 reporting, those hopes now seem fanciful. The company’s vaunted product pipeline, once a source of optimism, no longer looks like a game changer.

CEO Tavares Under Fire

One of the biggest casualties of this crisis could be Stellantis CEO Carlos Tavares, in my view. Historically, Tavares has been regarded as a sharp operator with a knack for turning around troubled automakers. But Tavares’ reputation is now under threat. Investors, myself included, are starting to question whether he can right the ship before his term ends in 2026​, following the exit of CFO and NAM COO. While I would welcome Tavares exit - after his horrible performance - I would be concerned about a potential management shake up, which would undoubtedly add additional uncertainty to the picture.

While we are addressing Stellantis management concerns, I point out that Stellantis reporting appears to be somewhat challenging to navigate as compared to competitors' reporting - with Stellantis being one of the few public listed companies that chooses to not report to its investors quarterly cash flow and margin data; with Stellantis reporting these insights only at H1 and H2. Moreover, I feel that management has a tendency to release vague and insufficiently detailed explanations for significant financial shifts (my personal opinion). For instance, in its 2024 profit warning, Stellantis provided broad reasons such as "remediation actions on North American performance issues" and "temporarily elevated working capital" without elaborating on how these factors specifically led to a projected cash flow swing from positive to a negative range of €5-10 billion​. Additionally, Stellantis bundles various financial impacts together, such as grouping operational issues in North America with global demand challenges, which muddies the picture and makes it hard for investors to assess the true underlying problems. The absence of granular, forward-looking data, particularly around revenue and cash flow projections, adds another layer of difficulty for analysts.

Lastly, I point out that Stellantis' past M&A activities don't help making financial analysis easier: Through the PSA and FCA merger, Stellantis has created a complex web of financial integrations that blend different accounting practices and reporting structures. This makes it somewhat difficult to assess the company's actual operational efficiency and the success (or failure) of the merger. The company frequently touts synergies and cost savings from these M&A activities, but the lack of detailed reporting on how these synergies are being realized adds to my confusion. Additionally, the adjustments related to restructuring costs, goodwill amortization, and non-recurring expenses complicate the financial picture, making it difficult for stakeholders to separate one-off M&A-related impacts from the core operational performance​.

Upside Risks

While I am very bearish on Stellantis, I acknowledge that the auto giant still has an enormous revenue base. If the macro backdrop improves aggressively, or Stellantis manages to cut more layers of costs, Free Cash Flow upside could swing to levels seen in 2023. This could resurface a value thesis for this struggling automaker. Moreover, Stellantis' dividend cut is far from certain as the company has plenty of cash on the balance sheet (some of which is working capital used in industrial business, however).

Final Thoughts

For all its past "success", Stellantis now faces a grim reality. The company's U.S. inventory issues have spiraled out of control, dragging down earnings and cash flow. Meanwhile, the Stellantis' European operations are also faltering. Investors, already disillusioned, should be skeptic towards this company, that seems to be lurching from one crisis to the next. On that note, Stellantis' issues look structural and systemic, not merely cyclical. The magnitude of the profit warning, combined with weakening demand, cash flow problems, likely incoming dividend cut, and a management team under fire, all make challenges impossible to dismiss. In my view, Stellantis stock is a "Strong Sell". And until cash flow for the company stabilizes over at least 2 years, I reject any attempt to assign a valuation/ target price.

data.title: Verizon: A Dividend Powerhouse With Vast Upside Potential
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bullish thesis about Verizon's stock (NYSE:VZ) aged well as the stock outperformed the broader U.S. market since June 20, delivering a 12.5% total return to investors.

I remain bullish from the long-term perspective and today want to focus mostly on the Q3 earnings preview since the earnings release is scheduled for Tuesday, October 22. Verizon has quite a strong earnings surprise record, which is obviously a positive sign. Moreover, this time consensus expectations seem to be quite conservative. Seasonality trends suggest that November and December are historically the most successful months for VZ, which is also quite bullish. Moreover, given VZ's high reliance on leverage, I expect the management to be more positive with their outlook given the Fed's recent interest rate cut. The above 6% dividend yield is still safe and there is a 40% upside potential. All in all, I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for VZ.

Recent developments

VZ released its latest quarterly earnings on July 22, missing consensus revenue and EPS estimates. Revenue grew YoY by 0.6% YoY, which was the best performance over the last six quarters. Operating income was almost flat YoY, but higher finance costs weighed on the EPS, which decreased from $1.21 to $1.15. That said, the EPS decline is explained by a non-operating factor, which means that the pullback is highly likely temporary.

Verizon's balance sheet is still highly leveraged as the company had aggressive CAPEX spending over the last few years as part of the 5G network build-out. On the other hand, the situation with the net debt improved sequentially by decreasing from $178.3 billion to $175.8 billion. I do not consider substantial leverage as a significant risk since debt maturity schedule looks quite comfortable for the company, and VZ' credit ratings from the world's leading agencies are decent.

Verizon demonstrates stable and strong profitability, which ensures a robust covered ratio. Moreover, the peak 5G build-out is in the rearview mirror and CAPEX is expected to moderate. To sum up, I still consider that the stock's 6.2% forward dividend yield is sustainable and safe.

The upcoming earnings release is scheduled for October 22, which is very soon from now. Wall Street analysts expect Q3 2024 revenue to be $33.44 billion, which will be 0.2% higher on a YoY basis. High finance costs will likely weigh on the bottom line once again as the adjusted EPS is expected to decline from $1.22 to $1.18. However, the positive sign is that the adjusted EPS is expected to improve sequentially, from $1.15 to $1.18. Wall Street analysts appear to be quite cautious regarding the upcoming earnings release as there were nine downward EPS revisions over the last 90 days.

The good part is that Verizon quite rarely disappoints against EPS consensus estimates. According to the history, there were only two negative EPS surprises over the last sixteen quarters, which is quite a robust record. On the other hand, VZ's negative revenue surprises occur frequently, but these misses look quite insignificant in relative terms.

I am optimistic about the upcoming earnings release based on the company's recent operational performance as well. The company has been demonstrating consistent growth in its wireless service revenue over the last several quarters. This strength is backed by the company's aggressive rollout of its 5G Ultra Wideband network, which aims to enhance service quality.

Verizon has been quite successful in building its competitive edge in 5G, as evidenced by the recognition of its service quality by industry experts. For instance, Verizon is notably ahead of its main rivals in terms of 5G speed and reliability, providing the best 5G experience for users, according to RootMetrics.

Verizon's commitment to delivering the best 5G experience is likely to enhance its competitive position, leading to improved customer acquisition. Moreover, we saw quite strong performance in Q2 when Verizon delivered a 2.5 million wireless retail postpaid phone gross adds [9.3% YoY growth], and 1.8 million consumer wireless retail postpaid phone gross adds [12.0% YoY growth]. Furthermore, in the previous earnings presentation, the management emphasized that improving customer experience through C-band deployment was the top priority for the second half of 2024. This focus suggests that Verizon is likely to report strong customer growth metrics in Q3 as well.

The stock more frequently reacted positively to earnings releases over the last eight quarters, according to my below compilation. On the other hand, the last two quarterly earnings releases were absorbed quite negatively by the market by notable after-earnings sell-offs. Given the several downward EPS revisions from consensus over the last 90 days, I believe expectations are already quite conservative. Therefore, there is a probability that the market could react positively to the upcoming earnings release if the company delivers positive revenue and EPS surprises.

Historical seasonality patterns also look quite bullish for the remainder of the year. November and December were historically the strongest months for VZ over the last decade with a 67% success rate. Since the company usually reports Q3 earnings closer to the end of October, such a historically strong performance in November suggests that investors more frequently have positive reactions to the company's Q3 earnings releases.

Recent interest rates cut by the Fed look quite positive for VZ. As we saw above, the balance sheet is highly leveraged, which weighs on the bottom line. Therefore, expected further interest rate cuts through 2025 appears to be a solid tailwind for the bottom line. The management might share a more positive outlook due to decreasing interest rates during the upcoming earnings call, which could also be a positive catalyst for the stock price.

Last but not least, I expect the management to share more details about the Frontier acquisition. At first glance, the deal looks quite promising for Verizon's shareholders. The deal will significantly help VZ to expand its broadband footprint across 25 U.S. states, which is significant. The management expects the merger to bring synergies of $500 million by enhancing operational efficiency. I think that VZ will be able to realize these synergies because we saw that it offers premium quality to customers, and it means that Frontier's clients will highly likely enjoy Verizon's premium offerings.

Valuation update

VZ's performance has been solid over the last 12 months with a 42% rally, and a 17% YTD share price appreciation. Most of the valuation ratios look quite attractive, mostly in line or lower than the sector median.

For a dividend stock like Verizon, the best way to proceed with my valuation analysis is the dividend discount model [DDM]. Cost of equity is the discount rate for the DDM approach, which I figure out below using the CAPM formula. All variables are easily available on the Internet.

Verizon's cost of equity is 6.41%, which will be used as a required rate of return for my DDM. The stock's next year dividend provided by consensus is $2.72, which is a reliable assumption given historical stability in payouts. A 2% dividend growth is in line with long-term inflation levels and Verizon's last five years' dividend CAGR.

The fair share price according to my DDM simulation is $61.72. This is 40% higher than the last close, meaning that Verizon is currently very attractively valued.

Risks update

Soaring revenues and EPS delivered by technological giants supported by the AI revolution made growth stocks extremely attractive for investors, while value stocks have been significantly lagging behind over the last few years. Since around a third of the S&P 500 index is represented by the Technological sector, VZ has been lagging the broader U.S. stock market over the last three years. There was only a relatively short period when VZ outperformed during the panic caused by accelerating inflation in the U.S. in early 2022.

With its above 6% forward dividend yield and deep discount, VZ represents a safe investment choice paying generous dividends. However, if the AI-fueled mania in growth continues for longer, the stock is poised to further underperform compared to the S&P 500.

Verizon's customers experienced two outages in one week, but the stock market did not react with any deep drawdowns. However, if outages occur again soon, this might raise doubts about Verizon's ability to provide stable services, potentially leading to significant reputational damage and eventually resulting in a stock selloff.

Bottom line

To conclude, VZ is still a "Strong Buy" before the Q3 earnings release. The company is likely to deliver solid performance against consensus estimates, which look quite conservative after multiple downward revisions. Verizon's above 6% dividend yield is still safe, and there is a 40% upside potential left.

data.title: The Key To Unlocking Gilts' Rebound
data.description:
By William Vaughan

In recent weeks, encouraging data on UK wages and inflation has emerged. Wage growth has slowed and, most recently, the Office for National Statistics reported that September inflation came in below the Bank of England's (BOE's) 2% target. UK government bonds are beginning to reverse their recent underperformance compared to US Treasuries and German bunds, but one major obstacle may stand in the way of gilts' rebound. In our view, the upcoming Autumn Budget on October 30 remains the key to unlocking sustained gilt outperformance.

Moderating inflation and wage growth typically would support the case for additional BOE rate cuts. However, investor concerns are rising over the possibility that the upcoming Budget could be more fiscally expansionary than expected, leading to increased gilt issuance and potentially slowing BOE rate cuts. Fueling the fears are reports that suggest Chancellor Rachel Reeves is seeking a buffer to ensure day-to-day spending is covered, with any borrowing directed toward investment projects. However, we believe the Chancellor is likely to prioritize market stability while actively seeking to avoid a "Truss-style" budget and the subsequent fallout. The Chancellor has talked about wanting to be fiscally tight, and now needs to back up those words. Any increase in non-investment spending will likely be offset by higher taxes to avoid unsettling the markets.

Since investment projects tend to be slower to implement and focus on boosting both supply and demand, increased capital spending would not be expected to significantly alter the Monetary Policy Committee's inflation outlook. As such, the Budget's impact on the BOE's policy direction may be limited. With gilts currently trading at wide spreads compared to bunds and Treasuries, we see a potential opportunity for this gap to narrow following the Budget announcement.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources Is Growing Its Assets
data.description:
Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ) is one of the largest pure-play upstream companies in Canada. The company has a market capitalization of more than US$75 billion and recently announced the acquisition of some Chevron (CVX) Canadian assets for US$ 6.5 billion. As we’ll see throughout this article, Canadian Natural Resources' intelligent acquisition and continued investments in its business make the company a valuable long-term investment.

Transaction Overview

The company is acquiring 20% in the Athabasca Oil Sands Project (“AOSP”) to bring the company’s working interest to 90% with 62.5 thousand barrels / day of SCO production. The company calls SCO production “no decline”, however, all assets do have an effective reserve life that’s worth noting. There will some day be a reserve replacement cost, however, the company might see the assets as long enough life that it’s unimportant.

The company is also acquiring a 70% working interest in the Duvernay play in Alberta, which is >40 thousand barrels / day is barrels of oil equivalent / day. The company is also receiving 100 thousand net acres of non-producing oil sands leases. Overall, the company is receiving 1.5 billion barrels of reserves and ~122.5 thousand barrels / day of production, which puts the reserve life of the assets at ~35 years.

The company is funding the acquisition through cash, which is exciting to see, versus dilution. It's an acquisition that the company can comfortably afford with strong synergies with its existing assets. That will help drive substantial shareholder returns.

Canadian Natural Resources Market

CNQ is the only Canadian oil company with more than 5 billion barrels of reserves, with an impressive portfolio of assets.

The company has SCO at ~35% which is a strong base for the company. The assets have ~0% decline, and the company’s overall corporate decline rate is ~11%. The company has ~42% conventional production and ~13% Pelican & thermal production. The company’s production is ~2.2 Bcf/d of natural gas, which makes it one of the largest natural gas producers in Canada, which combines with the company being the largest crude oil producer in Canada.

The company’s diversified assets are sufficient to maintain strong oil production for decades to come.

CNQ is targeting substantial development potential. The company’s natural gas production growth is doubling, and it sees potential liquids growth of almost 2 million barrels / day. It’s a tall order that the company has set for itself, and it requires a cooperative environment to achieve. Whether or not the company can manage to achieve that remains to be seen.

However, the company has the reserves and the long-life assets to be able to accomplish it. The company’s assumption of ~$70 / barrel WTI is something that it can afford comfortably, while natural gas prices remain in a tough position. Natural gas prices are expected to recover, supported by LNG, however, whether that truly happens remains to be seen.

Canadian Natural Resources Returns

The company's original forecast was based on net debt of $10 billion CAD to return 100% of FCF to shareholders. That threshold with the acquisition has increased to $12 billion, ($US 8.7 billion). It's still a level the company can easily achieve with its latest acquisition.

The company has recently increased its dividend with the acquisition. The company's new dividend is $2.25 / share CAD ($US 1.63). The company's new dividend is a more than 4.5% dividend yield, a strong yield that it can comfortably afford. That's on top of base capital and the company's continued shareholder returns.

In 2023, the company achieved a double-digit FCF yield. The company has the ability to continue these strong returns at $70 WTI, making the company a valuable long-term investment. This is an intelligent amount of asset growth.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is simply crude oil prices. The company is incredibly profitable with high WTI prices, but below ~$80 WTI is where the company becomes less profitable. That’s above current prices, and there remains substantial risks to long-term oil prices. That can hurt the company’s ability to continue generating market beating returns.

Conclusion

Canadian Natural Resources has a strong portfolio of low-decline oil sands assets. The company is making an intelligent acquisition with Chevron's assets, which integrates well with the company's existing assets. That will enable the company to grow its returns, albeit with a temporary pause to fix its balance sheet.

However, the company's balance sheet overall is quite strong. The company will continue paying its almost 5% dividend yield, repurchase shares, and improve its balance sheets. Overall, that makes the company a valuable investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: First Horizon: Solid Q3 Results Leave Shares With More Upside Potential
data.description:
Shares of First Horizon (NYSE:FHN) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 66% as the company has recovered strongly from the early 2023 regional banking crisis. I last covered First Horizon in December of last year, rating shares a “buy,” given its solid capital position. Since then, the stock has returned a solid 33%, outpacing the market’s 27% gain. With new financials, now is a good time to revisit FHN. I remain bullish.

In the company’s third quarter, First Horizon earned $0.42, beating consensus by $0.04 as revenue rose by 6% from last year. The bank has seen some margin pressure as it has turned to higher cost deposits to fund growth, though I expect this pressure to fade over the next two quarters. Importantly, after a one-time item last year, FHN’s underwriting has returned to its strong standard, resulting in encouraging credit performance.

Non-interest income jumped to $200 million, up $27 million from last year and $14 million from Q2. Its fixed income origination team performed particularly well as investors sought to lock in rates ahead of anticipated Fed easing. FHN earned $593k in daily fixed income revenue, up 22% from last year, and this pace will likely moderate at some point. Wealth management units rose 18% from last year to $39 million, and given ongoing market strength, I anticipate further gains here.

Looking next at interest rate trends, net interest income declined by $2 million sequentially to $631 million, though it was still up 4% from last year. That year-over-year growth largely reflects growth in the business and the movement away from higher cost wholesale funding. FHN’s net interest margin (NIM) compressed 7bps to 3.31%, still 14bps higher than last year, given an improved funding mix. Disappointingly, even as the Fed reduced interest rates, its deposit costs rose. FHN paid 3.44% on interest-bearing deposit, up 14bps from Q2. Given a lower average fed funds rate, I would like to see this flat or down slightly.

As you can see below, First Horizon increased the use of brokered deposits, which are more expensive than client deposits. This $800 million growth was a 5bp headwind to NIM. Overall, period-end deposits did rise by $1.8 billion sequentially. I was encouraged to see that noninterest-bearing deposits are essentially flat, and client deposit growth has been strong.

$1 billion of brokered CDs mature in Q4, and this should be an opportunity to reset some deposit costs 50+bps lower, essential to protecting NIM. More meaningfully, FHN has $18 billion of deposits with promotional pricing that are eligible for reductions in Q4. FHN will need to balance rate cuts against client retention. So far, retention has been strong at 91%. With over 35% of its core deposit base ending promotional pricing, there should be a path to meaningfully lower funding costs and expand NIM at least by Q1 2025.

On the asset side, loans declined by $300 million sequentially due to the sale of a health care lending portfolio of $340 million. Its commercial real estate exposure was broadly stable, and I expect to see some declines next year as many outstanding loans have been fully funded, and new originations are muted.

Even with the Fed rate cut, loan yields rose by 3bps to 6.37%, which reflects higher origination yields. Still, we are likely to see declines in coming quarters because 56% of loans are floating rate. Offsetting this, First Horizon has $4 billion of fixed rate loans at 4.6% and $1 billion of securities at 2.2% that mature next year. Reinvesting these proceeds at higher market yields should help to dampen the impact of lower yields on floating assets.

Overall, FHN has a $9.4 billion securities portfolio at a 2.58% yield, which means FHN has a $1 billion unrealized loss given higher yields today. With a ~4.6 year duration, we should see this unrealized loss decline over time. Because FHN has just $83 billion in assets, it also does not need to phase these losses into its capital calculations. Over 10% of the portfolio matures over the next year, so we should see this portfolio yield gradually rise.

Turning to credit quality, FHN provisioned $35 million for credit losses, down $20 million from Q2 and down from $110 million last year. Last year, it took a $72 million loss on one loan where it has sought legal recourse. My view then was that this was truly a one-time slip-up, given FHN’s history of robust underwriting. With another year of data, that appears to be the case.

FHN took just 0.15% charge-offs in Q3, down from 22bps in Q2. As a result, management took an $11 million reserve build, down from $21 million last quarter. This includes $8 million of reserves from Hurricane Helene. While it is not clear there will be any losses, management has chosen to be proactive, consistent with their conservative lending practices, in my view.

Just given the aging of the economic cycle, FHN is still seeing an increase in NPLs relative to last year, though as you can see the pace slowed considerably at just $4 million in Q3. Given reserve builds over the past year, it now has a 1.44% allowances to loan ratio. At 1.5x, coverage of NPLs is a bit lower than I like to see, though given limited unsecured consumer exposure, it can run with lower reserves. Still, I expect to see modest ongoing reserve builds.

Partly offsetting lower reserves, FHN is running with elevated capital levels. It has an 11.2% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio, above its 11% near-term target. This 11% target is also above its long-term capital needs, likely in the 10-10.5% range, recognizing the macroeconomic uncertainty as well as unrealized losses on securities that are excluded from its capital calculations.

Given its strong capital position, FHN has executed $441 million of buybacks this year in its $650 million program. That included $75 million in Q3. Thanks to these repurchases, its share count is down 4% over the past year. The remaining authorization will cover Q4 repurchases, and the board will revisit the buyback in early 2025. If the economic outlook remains resilient, management could lower its capital target, freeing up more buyback capacity.

Looking to Q4, we should see NII grow as it benefits from deposit repricing, and management expects similar credit trends as seen in Q3. One limiting factor for buyback growth will be loan growth, and FHN is likely to balance capital needed to support the business with shareholder returns. Additionally with $83 billion of assets, FHN will eventually (likely in 2027) hit the $100 billion mark that leads to more regulatory oversight. There are likely to be ongoing expenses as FHN prepares. Essentially, I expect FHN to run itself like a $100 billion institution as it gets past $90 billion, even though it legally does not have to in order to ensure a smooth transition.

FHN continues to deliver strong results, and I expect to see NIM stabilize by Q1 2025, as long as the Fed is done with 50bp rate cuts at single meetings, as I expect is the case. I also do not expect to see further credit deterioration beyond current levels of provisions. As such, FHN has about $1.60 in go forward earnings power. With shares just below 11x earnings, I view the bank as attractive given its underlying growth and excess capital position. I can see shares moving past 12x earnings like large peers or past $19. Combined with its dividend, that provides over 10% upside. As such, I remain a buy on FHN.

data.title: CorMedix: Delayed Approval, Low Cash, Good Prospects
data.description:
CorMedix Corp (NASDAQ:CRMD) has been around for nearly 2 decades, but only last year got its product DefenCath approved by the FDA. DefenCath is an antimicrobial catheter lock solution (CLS) which is approved to reduce the incidence of catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSIs). CRBSI is a chronic and highly prevalent problem faced by hemodialysis patients who need to use these catheters for the dialysis process.

In their corporate presentation, CorMedix notes that around 80% of kidney failure patients begin hemodialysis with a CVC, or a central venous catheter, and about 20% remain reliant on CVCs long-term, where the average duration of CVC usage could be 220 days. All of this produces a high risk of CRBSI in CVC-dependent hemodialysis patients, to the tune of 70%. According to a 2017 report, in these patients, the incidence of CRBSI ranges from a quarter to a third of these patients. It may cost up to $3.4bn to treat these patients, and there’s a threefold higher mortality risk. It may cost up to $63k per patient for hospitalization related to CRBSI. This is where DefenCath positions itself, as the first and only FDA-approved antimicrobial catheter lock solution.

How it works, and trial data

DefenCath consists of two ingredients, taurolidine, a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, and heparin, an anticoagulant. The former has strong activity against both multi-drug resistant bacteria, and fungi.

The phase 3 LOCK-IT-100 study demonstrated DefenCath’s efficacy against CRBSI. Data showed that DefenCath was able to reduce the risk of CRBSIs by 71% versus heparin CLS alone. Interestingly, this data was announced way back in 2018, which is a generation ago in biotech terms.

The CRBSI market

The CRBSI market size is estimated at $2.6bn in 2023. The market is increasing in size due to the increasing usage of catheters to treat patients undergoing chemotherapy, hemodialysis or parenteral nutrition. When infections occur, they are standardly treated by antibiotics. However, the recurrence of these infections may cause antibiotic resistance, so a preventive measure like a CLS is critical.

Current standard of care is heparin-based antibiotic CLS, used with drugs like vancomycin. However, as the trial data I cited has shown, DefenCath proved its superiority over heparin CLS in a large number of patients and with high statistical significance.

CorMedix history

CorMedix has had a long and tortuous history of over 17 years. During this time, it has undergone, as FiercePharma says it, “a reorganization, a CEO change, an exit from Europe, two complete response letters and a switch of manufacturers and suppliers.” My earlier articles covered some of this history of CorMedix' arduous path to approval. DefenCath’s approval was a long-fought battle, but at the end of it, using a less-travelled regulatory pathway called Limited Population Pathway for Antibacterial and Antifungal Drugs, DefenCath became the only approved drug for CRBSI. The approval comes with a QIDP (Qualified Infectious Disease Program) designation, which means an additional five years of exclusivity. Here’s what the company says about this:

DefenCath has 10 years of market exclusivity pursuant to FDA approval as a New Chemical Entity (NCE, 5 years) and Qualified Infectious Disease Product (QIDP, 5 years) and has the potential to receive an additional 6 months upon completion of a pediatric hemodialysis study, as well as IP protection covering the product through 2042.

DefenCath has been approved for both inpatient and outpatient populations. It has inpatient reimbursement through NTAP and outpatient reimbursement through TDAPA for Medicare patients. NTAP is New Technology Add-on Payment, which is designed to reimburse up to 65% of the cost of a new technology beyond additional reimbursement, for new medical technologies and treatments under the Medicare Inpatient Prospective Payment System (IPPS). TDAPA is Transitional Drug Add-On Payment Adjustment, which provides additional payment for certain new drugs or biological products used for end-stage renal disease (ESRD) patients, which goes beyond the ESRD Prospective Payment System (PPS).

Market, revenue and financials

The company estimates that the total addressable market for outpatient ESRD patients could be ~37mn vials, while it could be 3.8mn vials for inpatients. This represents 170k outpatient ESRD patients who use a CVC, and the TDAPA provides an incentive for dialysis clinics to adopt the drug outside the typical End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) bundled payment system.

There are three phases in the TDAPA reimbursement process. The first phase, during TDAPA, is for years 0 to 2. During this time, Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) offers additional reimbursement to dialysis clinics, on top of the normal ESRD bundled payment. For the first 6 months, reimbursement is based on the Wholesale Acquisition Cost (WAC), currently set at $249.99 per vial of DefenCath. From the 7th to the 24th month, reimbursement moves to Average Sales Price (ASP), which is reported quarterly to CMS. Over time, ASP is expected to decline as gross-to-net adjustments and discounts are applied.

The post-TDAPA period is divided into years 3 to 5, and beyond 5 years. In the first period post-TDAPA, an add-on payment is still provided, but at a 35% discount to the prior year’s total expenditure for DefenCath, divided by the total number of dialysis treatments, regardless of whether DefenCath was used in every treatment. In the 5+ year period, DefenCath becomes bundled with the normal ESRD bundled payment system.

CRMD has a market cap of $603mn and a cash balance of $45mn. They have only reported DefenCath revenues for a partial quarter, so the next earnings will be important, and we will not discuss the prior earnings here. About its expenses, the company says: that OpEx in Q2 2024 was $15.6mn, where Selling and Marketing was $4.1mn, and G&A was $3.8mn. These figures have increased hugely from the same quarter in the previous year. Research and development expenses were approximately $4.1mn. At that burn rate, they have a cash runway of 3–4 quarters - from June - so they have just around 2 quarters of cash left now. That is an unsustainable figure, and I expect a dilution or other type of coercive funding very soon.

Risks

CRMD has been decimated by the approval delay, which can be seen reflected in their very small cash reserve. I believe DefenCath will put them in a better position over the next 3–4 quarters, however, their plans to start trials for their next indication, which is CRBSI in adult Total Parenteral Nutrition, or TPN, will need additional funds.

Bottomline

CRMD has more than doubled since July/August when it launched DefenCath. This spurt has been caused by the launch, however, if there’s further spike, that will be caused only by the earnings because there is no other catalyst ahead. Earnings are difficult to predict, especially since the first reported quarter had partial data. I think we should wait for the Q3 earnings data before taking a call. Admittedly, that is a low risk/reward strategy because, if the Q3 figures are robust, CRMD stock may well become unreachable.

data.title: Default Risk Eases For Most U.S. Market Sectors In Q3 2024
data.description:
Default risk for most public US companies ticked lower at the end of the third quarter compared to the second quarter.

Median probability of default scores dropped across seven of 11 market sectors, with the remaining four - healthcare, utilities, real estate and financials - all roughly flat as of Sept. 30 compared to June 30, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence's RiskGauge model.

The scores represent the median odds of default on debt within a year and are based on financial reports and the volatility of share prices for public companies on major US exchanges, accounting for country- and industry-related risks and other macroeconomic factors.

Global corporate defaults are slowing in 2024 compared to 2023, with 67 defaults by US companies in the first nine months of 2024, 10 fewer than the same period a year earlier, according to S&P Global Ratings data as of Oct. 16. Ratings does not contribute to or participate in the generation of Market Intelligence's RiskGauge scores.

Most, least vulnerable

Oil and gas equipment and services companies and publishers were the industries with the highest median probability of default scores at the end of the quarter. For the publishing industry, this is at least the third quarter in a row that the industry has landed at or near the top of most-vulnerable industries, according to Market Intelligence data.

Self-storage REITs registered the lowest median probability of default score among US industries, again ranking among the least vulnerable industries. Regional banks ranked second on the least-vulnerable industries as measured by Market Intelligence's RiskGauge model, though the median default risk score fell from the prior quarter.

Biggest increases, drops

At the industry level, median probability of default scores moved 1 percentage point or less during the quarter. The greatest rise was for diversified financial services, with a jump to 4.2% from 3.2% at the end of June. Healthcare services followed, also moving to 4.2%, yet the move was a narrower 0.8-percentage point increase.

The fall in median probability of default scores was smaller than the rise, with the greatest drop at 0.7 percentage points for apparel retail companies to 7.7% at the end of the third quarter. That was a similar fall to footwear and oil and gas refining and marketing companies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Origin Materials: Attractive Discount To Net Assets
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Origin Materials (NASDAQ:ORGN) is still struggling with significant cash burn, and their current fundamentals still signal some early-stage financial weakness. Despite efforts to reorganize and restructure the business, I still think their company has a long ways to go before becoming profitable. The overall business is quite innovative and a potential disruptor to sustainable materials, giving investors a high-risk, high reward play in their portfolio. However, the stock trades at a significant discount to tangible book value of $2.65 which presents a potential speculative buying opportunity.

Company Overview

Origin Materials "is a leading technology company with a mission to enable the world’s transition to sustainable materials", according to their annual report. They focus on replacing "petroleum-based materials with decarbonized materials in a wide range of end products, such as food and beverage packaging, clothing, textiles, plastics, car parts, carpeting, tires, adhesives, soil amendments, fuels, and more".

In particular, the company is on track to making PET caps through their proprietary biomass conversion technology. They describe this technology in their 10-K,

Our biomass conversion technology is capable, using wood feedstocks, of producing CMF and HTC with a negative carbon impact. We believe these chemicals can replace petroleum-based inputs, lowering the carbon footprint of a wide range of materials without increasing cost or sacrificing performance.

This company is quite the innovator and disruptor, and has early-stage fundamentals similar to most startups. I think this makes this stock a high risk, high reward play for risk tolerant investors. If successful, their company boasts a total addressable market of "$1 trillion" according to their investor presentation, which could give dramatic upside. It's certainly watchlist worthy and worth potentially buying more on pullbacks.

Furthermore, their biomass conversion technology is extremely impressive and potentially game-changing. The core of biomass technology is to use "wood residues, agricultural waste, wood waste" and turn it into "products that are currently made from fossil feedstocks such as petroleum and natural gas". This would help companies meet carbon-neutral goals and be more environmentally sustainable.

While still in the early stages, the train is already starting to move. Recently, in the second quarter, the company announced "signed customer memorandum of understanding for over $100 Million of PET Caps". This positive news has already been priced in for now, but suggests that the company is moving forward in the right direction.

Overall, the business is an early-stage, strong potential disruptor that has innovative biomass conversion technology. As a high-risk, high reward stock, it would be most suitable for the risk tolerant and patient investor. I rate shares as a speculative buy, as I think the need for sustainable materials is likely to increase and Origin Materials does trade at a nice discount to tangible book value.

Potential TAM Is Massive, And So Is The Competition

Many businesses and consumers seem to be moving towards a sustainable future, emphasizing carbon neutrality and environmentally friendly products. As such, the TAM that Origin Materials has is quite massive, and Origin happens to be one of the few players that has succeeded with biomass conversion technology. If successful, it is not unreasonable to suggest that Origin Materials may become a billion-dollar company if they capture even a modest percentage of the total market.

According to their investor presentation, they think the TAM is potentially $1 trillion.

They also claim that the PET caps alone have a TAM of $65 billion, and that they will be one of the first to this big market. I think that while the TAM may be big, the competition may be equally fierce. Big markets tend to attract big players. A handful of research and development companies are developing technology that may compete against Origin, such as Newlight Technologies and Novomer. These competitors also try to use innovative technology to create sustainable material from waste.

While the market is big, so is the competition. I think that Origin Materials has their work cut out from them especially as they seem to have limited resources at the moment. With significant cash burn and no profitability yet, it's a race to the finish line to capture market share. At this price, it's mostly a speculative bet that management can reign cash burn and turn profitable in a few years.

Reducing Cash Burn Is Critical

The company is actively working on reducing cash burn to manage their cash flow carefully to avoid having to raise capital through equity dilution. They maintain that no equity capital raise is necessary, as they plan to use the proceeds from making PET caps to run their business and invest in more research and development.

So far, the recent news suggests that the cash burn is getting better. According to a recent press release,

The product already generated at Origin 1 over multiple campaigns, as well as materials Origin may purchase from third-party suppliers, can enable us to advance scale-up while allowing us to reduce our current overall cash burn.

The Company anticipates that it will incur approximately $0.6 million in restructuring charges in connection with the roughly 28% workforce reduction, consisting of cash expenditures of approximately $0.5 million and non-cash expenses of approximately $0.1 million.

I expect the cash burn to steadily diminish, buying Origin Materials more time to scale up production of their PET caps and develop their existing biomass technology. The PET Caps business is to me a good short-term boost to prop up the business, but it doesn't seem to be a long-term play. Most people I observe drink out of their own designated water bottles with no caps, so the $65 billion market does not seem to be growing that quickly to me.

I'm liking what I see so far, and would recommend a potential entry point on pullbacks in the share price. It's still a long road ahead, but the train is finally getting moving, with PET caps finally entering the market. The stock price has already priced this positive news in, yet still trades at a discount to tangible book. Therefore, with a more lean business, a product entering market, and a healthy cash and marketable securities position of $130 million as of June 30, 2024, things are looking a lot better than before.

Valuation - $2.50 Fair Value

Valuing this business is tricky, as it is still high risk/high reward. If the business is successful in capturing a modest percentage of the $65 billion in TAM, then it could be a potential billion-dollar company. That would imply a multi-bagger return for investors. However, if the business fails, then investors may face a potential wipe out. Therefore, it is hard to give a price target because it depends on how successful the company is in managing cash burn and attaining sales.

I will assume that sales reach $50 million annually for the next few years, as suggested in the press release,

Therefore, to fulfill demand for this MOU as well as anticipated demand from other customers, we expect to build capacity well beyond our initial system purchases, which we previously announced as having expected capacity to generate between $45 million and $65 million in annual revenue.

Based on sales, the company is probably worth 4x sales, due to its large TAM potential and unique proprietary technology. So, we can assume that the fair value based on sales is probably $200 million, at least for now. Divide by shares outstanding of 141 million gets me $1.4 per share fair value.

However, based on tangible book, the stock is likely worth much more. According to Seeking Alpha, the tangible book value is $2.65 according to the latest report. Most of the book is made up of cash, marketable securities, and net PPE. Those machinery and production plants have value, and are likely relatively new. Therefore, it is safe to say that this new PPE is probably worth a book value of $237.7 million, and overall the tangible book value is a reasonable indicator of the fair value of the stock.

Therefore, I think the stock is a good speculative buy based on both a sales multiple and book value valuation approach. The business has gotten better, has visible track to sales growth, and can likely manage its cash burn over time. I maintain a price target of $2.50, which is slightly conservative to the tangible book value.

Risks

The business is still in very early stages and does face some chance of running out of money. Cash is still burning at $50+ million annually, and if it continues, then management may change their guidance and have to issue equity. This dilution can hurt shareholder value. Also, the company will likely lose money for the next few years, so don't expect earnings anytime soon.

After the $100 million of PET caps are made and sold for the MOU, management may find difficulty in finding new customers. So their investments in PPE and production capability may be wasted temporarily, as no new customer wants PET caps. Then, sales would go back down and really hurt cash burn.

Competition is brutal, and there are many players fighting for this large, trillion dollar market. Origin Materials is going up against bigger players with more resources and perhaps reputation. Also, the switch away from fossil-fuel materials may take a while, and slow down the potential of Origin Materials.

Buy Origin Materials

I like what's happening to this business, and I do think it's worth a speculative buy rating. The company has succeeded in making PET caps, which are one of a kind and also seemingly in high demand. Pretty soon, the world may see their water bottles and beverages all have these new PET caps on them. In a $65 billion market, management seems ready to turn the business profitable in the long term, and the discount to tangible book does provide some margin of safety for speculative investors.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Quantitative Tightening Continues
data.description:
The Federal Reserve lowered its policy rate of interest in the middle of September 2024, by 50 basis points.

It promised to continue to keep its policy of quantitative tightening going...although at a reduced rate...as it continued to move into the future.

So, this "new" period of quantitative tightening has begun and the Fed has kept its promise.

Thus, in the first phase of quantitative tightening, from March 16, 2022, to September 18, 2024, the Federal Reserve reduced its "securities held outright" by $1,804.4 billion or by $1.8 trillion.

Since September 18, 2024, the Federal Reserve has reduced its "securities held outright", by $44.1 billion.

Round 2 of the Fed's quantitative tightening has begun.

Since the end of the Great Recession and before the quantitative tightening began, the Federal Reserve conducted four rounds of quantitative easing.

Overall the "excess reserves" of the commercial banking system, otherwise labeled "Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks," have roughly remained constant.

Little else can be said at this time about the Fed's policy except that the Fed is continuing to act as it stated it would. Thus, the Fed continues to build trust in financial markets.

The one thing that has been happening that has been of some concern to the analysts is that the M2 money supply has remained relatively constant for some time.

The concern is that this will result in slower growth for the economy...or, even a recession.

But, as I have been arguing for some time now, we consider the buildup of bank liquidity to combat the recession, the banking system has plenty of "cash on hand."

Here is the picture of the M2 money stock growth since the first of 2020.

If one looks at the chart from a March 2022 date, one sees that there has been a decline in the growth of the M2 money stock.

However, if one begins early in 2020, the M2 money stock has actually grown by 27.3 percent from then up to the present date.

The compound annual rate of growth during this particular period of time is over 7.0 percent.

No one really questions the fact that a compound annual rate of growth in excess of 7 percent is a very expansive rate of increase. Inflationary even.

And, this is why the excess reserves in the commercial banking system are around $3.3 trillion.

But, the other factor in this equation is that the velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock declined substantially during this period of time. Money was pumped into the banking system, but the money did not enter the "real" circuit of the economy...the money stayed in the "financial" circuit.

Thus, the "cash" is there...in the banking system...but the "cash" has not been used to purchase goods and services.

Thus, the Federal Reserve has seen inflationary pressures build up as the M2 money stock increased, but it did not see a sustained period of inflation because the money did not get into the spending circuit.

This is one of the things that the Federal Reserve is now fighting with its second round of quantitative tightening.

The Federal Reserve still has a ways to go to get the banking system back to a place where there are not lots and lots of cash lying around the bank vaults.

The Federal Reserve must still remove securities from the banking system so that all the "excess" cash is removed and "normal" business can move along without the fear that the "excess" cash hanging around will turn into another inflationary threat.

The Federal Reserve has done a real good job up to the present time.

Let's hope that it can continue to finish the job so that the banking system can return to a more normal position.

Here we see the Fed account "Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve Banks," something I refer to as "excess reserves."

You can see here the rise in these "excess reserves" as the Federal Reserve added, aggressively, securities to its securities portfolio.

Obviously, this figure shot up as the 2020 recession took place, and then even increased further as the economy moved into 2021 and 2022.

With the beginning of quantitative tightening, these reserve balances began to decline.

As can be seen in the latter half of 2023 these reserve balances increased somewhat as the Federal Reserve responded to the bank failures that took place during this time.

But, these reserve balances have not been trending downward once again up to the present time.

Readers should keep a close watch on this series going forward.

The main policy tool of the Federal Reserve is what it is doing to its holdings of securities.

The series on reserve balances should be watched to see how this series moves with any Federal Reserve efforts to manage its policy rate of interest.

This is the second part of the Fed's policy approach.

Yes, the Fed is in a stage of quantitative tightening.

But, the Fed has lowered its policy rate of interest and has indicated that, in the future, it would be looking at whether or not it should reduce its policy rate of interest further.

Here we need to watch what is happening to the reserve balances account, the "excess reserves" account.

Here we need to be aware of the pressure commercial banks are feeling on the excess cash they are carrying on their balance sheets.

Listen to what the Fed is saying, but also watch what the Fed is causing to happen.

Given the state of the U.S. economy, Federal Reserve officials may not be as anxious to further lower the Fed's policy rate of interest as it has talked about in recent weeks.

The Fed's policy rate of interest may stay higher for a longer period of time than market participants might think.

Stay tuned.
